coredata.dc:title: Green ratiometric fluorescent dual-mode nanosensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of new coccine in food
coredata.dc:creator: Tu, Yujiao
Liu, Ze
Jiang, Lei
Xiang, Yingying
Song, Fei
Meng, Lifen
Ji, Xiuling
Yuan, Lin
coredata.dc:description: New coccine is one of the most used red colorants in foods. Due to its cytotoxicity, quantitative determination is very necessary. Herein, the ratiometric fluorescent and UV absorption dual-mode nanosensor, derived from fangchinoline and o-phenylenediamine, was successfully prepared by hydrothermal way. The prepared carbon dots with three colors were purified with dialysis, thin layer chromatography and chromatographic column. Highly green fluorescence of Fan,N-CDs was quenched by new coccine with inner filter effect. The dependence of the ratiometric fluorescent response on new coccine concentrations was found linear over the range of 0–12.5 μM and 20–55 μM with LOD of 30.12 nM and 54.85 nm, respectively. The linearity of UV absorption ranged from 0 to 45 μM with of LOD 1.98 nM. In addition, the nanosensor was used to determine new coccine in energy drinks, carbonated beverage, gum and ketchup. It provides a new insight on the detection of new coccine by the dual-mode nanosensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: New coccine
Ratiometric fluorescent
Detection
Inner filter effect
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching test of new coccine
Quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime measurement
Sensitivity of new coccine detection based on dual-mode nanosensor
Possible quenching mechanisms of new coccine detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow map of preparation and purification of Fan,N-CDs.
(a) TEM (inset: HR-TEM) and (b) FTIR spectrum (c) XRD pattern of Fan,N-CDs.
(a) XPS full scan spectrum of the crude Fan,N-CDs. High-resolution C1s spectra (b), N1s spectra (c), and O1s spectra (d).
UV–vis, excitation and emission spectra of CDs1 (a), CDs2 (b), and Fan,N-CDs (c). Emission of Fan,N-CDs at various excitation wavelengths (d).
Fluorescence quenching efficiency of (a) metal ions, anion standard solution and food additives, (b) l-amino acids, vitamins and food additives to Fan,N-CDs.

Fluorescence spectra of Fan,N-CDs with various new coccine concentrations (0–62.5 μM) (a). The linear relationship between ratiometric fluorescent value (F515/F404) of Fan,N-CDs and new coccine concentrations (0–12.5 μM, 20–55 μM) (b). UV absorption spectra of Fan,N-CDs with various new coccine concentratiosn (0–45 μM) (c). The linear relationship between UV absorption rate ((A−A0)/A0) of Fan,N-CDs and new coccine concentratiosn (0–45 μM) (d).

UV–vis absorption of new coccine, Fan,N-CDs, Fan,N-CDs + new coccine (a) and decay lifetime of Fan,N-CDs, Fan,N-CDs + different concentration new coccine (b). UV–vis absorption of new coccine and Ex, Em of Fan,N-CDs (c).
Comparison of LOD on new coccine determination in the literature.
New coccine determine by Fan,N-CDs and HPLC in real food samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Green ratiometric fluorescent dual-mode nanosensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of new coccine in food

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Medical School, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650500, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: New coccine is one of the most used red colorants in foods. Due to its cytotoxicity, quantitative determination is very necessary. Herein, the ratiometric fluorescent and UV absorption dual-mode nanosensor, derived from fangchinoline and o-phenylenediamine, was successfully prepared by hydrothermal way. The prepared carbon dots with three colors were purified with dialysis, thin layer chromatography and chromatographic column. Highly green fluorescence of Fan,N-CDs was quenched by new coccine with inner filter effect. The dependence of the ratiometric fluorescent response on new coccine concentrations was found linear over the range of 0–12.5 μM and 20–55 μM with LOD of 30.12 nM and 54.85 nm, respectively. The linearity of UV absorption ranged from 0 to 45 μM with of LOD 1.98 nM. In addition, the nanosensor was used to determine new coccine in energy drinks, carbonated beverage, gum and ketchup. It provides a new insight on the detection of new coccine by the dual-mode nanosensor.

Schematic of the synthetic and purification route for fluorescent Fan,N-CDs and the applications as dual-mode for detection of New Coccine.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As important food additives, food colorants have been added during the manufacture of products such as cakes, cheese, soft drinks, medicine and cosmetics for improving appearance and flavour to attract consumers [1,2]. Food colorants, usually, are divided into natural and synthetic colorants [3]. Natural colorants are derived from the animal, plant and microorganism, such as carotenoids, carotene, riboflavin, chlorophyll and betalain, which benefit to the human health within daily dietary allowance, but harm human health when consumed excessively [4]. Due to stability, low cost, easy handling, and easy control over the intensity, synthetic colorants are preferred by food industries. The raw materials for synthetic colorants are mainly chemical products, and they are composed of azo dyes and non azo dyes. Some azo dyes, containing aromatic rings and NN bonds, can be cleaved into aromatic amines, which can trigger cancer [5].

New coccine (C20H11N2Na3O10S3, E124), also named Ponceau 4R, Acid Red 18, an azo dye, is one of the most used red colorants in foods, cosmetics, fruit juice, soft drinks, meat and textiles due to high stability [6]. However, overconsumption of azo dyes may cause hyperactivity to physical and mental effect on children, and allergies to adults [7,8]. The permissible daily intake for of new coccine is 0.025–0.5 g/kg. Therefore, a simple, reliable, low-cost, and sensitive analytical method is needed to determinate amount of new coccine in foods for the quality control, food safety, and human health.

Generally, traditional analytical methods are employed for the detection and analysis of food additives, for example high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), UV–vis and near-infrared (NIR), etc. [9–12]. It is widely believed UV–vis is a good technique because of its low cost and high efficiency. However, due to the interference from other compounds, UV–vis shows unsatisfactory selectivity [13–15]. NIR is extensive due to the sensitive detector. Raman analysis is fast and economic, with simple samples preparation except for low analytical sensitivity [16]. HPLC with high efficiency, high sensitivity, and good sample recovery rate, but requires expensive instrument and specialized operator, and produces toxic material from organic solvents. Similar to UV–vis spectroscopy, fluorescence is frequently more sensitive than UV–vis. Thus, fluorescence is chosen as an ideal tool to detect colorants content in food owing to its high selectivity and easy operation [17].

Due to small size, simple preparation and stable fluorescence, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) has attracted mounting number of attention [18]. They have been widely used for detecting heavy metal ions, environmental pollution, and as vectors for the drug delivery [19–22]. In this paper, ratiometric fluorescent and UV–vis absorption dual-mode nanosensor were employed to detect the food colorant new coccine. It is characterized by bright green fluorescence, low synthesis cost, high selectivity, and was used to determine new coccine in real food samples.

All analytical reagents, used in this study, were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), including fangchinoline, o-phenylenediamine (oPD), tartrazine (E102), dimethyl formamide (DMF), l-proline, new coccine (E124), l-aspartic acid, HgCl2, l-phenylalanine, rhodamine B (RbB), l-isoleucine, MgCl2, l-glutamine, trypan blue (TB), l-valine, BaCl2, l-histidine, riboflavin (E101), CoCl2, l-tryptophan, amaranth (E123), l-alanine, congo red (CR), l-methionine, Ni(NO3)2, l-threonine, methanol, AgNO3, propanol, CaCl2, ethanol, l-glutamate, FeCl3, acetone, Bi, l-arginine, KCl, l-cysteine, Vitamin B3, l-lysine, Vitamin C, l-glycine, CuSO4, Vitamin E, l-serine, l-tyrosine,Pb(NO3)2 and l-leucine. Standard solutions, such as phosphate, fluorine, nitrite, sulfate, acetate, nitrate, chlorine, iodine, bicarbonate, bromine and carbonate, were purchased from Xiamen Haibao Technology Co., Ltd. (Xiamen, China).

The fluorescence CDs were prepared with oPD and fangchinoline as raw materials via simple hydrothermal way. The oPD is one of the most important precursors to synthesize CDs due to as the carbon source, the nitrogen source and possessing a conjugated structure [23]. A variety of CDs with red, blue, yellow, and green fluorescence have been synthesized with oPD [24]. The fangchinoline contains the polyphenyl ring and polyamino, thus CDs synthesized with fangchinoline showing the characteristics of multicolor luminescence. Briefly, 2 g fangchinoline and 1 g oPD were dissolved in 20 mL MeOH, mixed, and subsequently transferred to the polytetrafluoroethylene high temperature reactor (200 °C, 8 h). After natural cooling, the products were dialyzed by the dialysis tube with molecular weight reduced by 1 KDa in H2O for 48 h, and then subjected to freeze-drying treatment.

The crude CDs were subjected to silica gel column chromatography, and then gradient eluted by CH2Cl2/MeOH (V/V, 5:1–0:1) added with 0.1% triethylamine. Three fractions were get based on the fluorescence colors: CDs1 with blue fluorescence, CDs2 with blue-green fluorescence, and CDs3 with green fluorescence. The preliminarily purified CDs3 were obtained by solvent concentration under vacuum, and the CDs3, namely Fan,N-CDs. Fan,N-CDs were characterized by TEM (Tecnai G2 F30S, FEI,Netherlands), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. U.S.A.), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, TENSOR 27, Bruker, Germany) and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD).

For further purification, the preliminarily purified Fan,N-CDs were subjected to silica gel column chromatography. They were gradient eluted by CH2Cl2/MeOH (V/V, 10:1–0:1) added with 0.1% triethylamine. Directed by thin layer chromatography, the secondarily purified Fan,N-CDs with green fluorescence were obtained, and detected by Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectrometer (Bruker AM-400), with chemical shifts in ppm rel. to Me4Si.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching test of new coccine
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 12 μL of purified Fan,N-CDs solution (1 mg/mL) was added with different concentrations of new coccine solution 100 μL, and then diluted to 4.0 mL with ddH2O. The ratiometric fluorescent value (F515/F404) of Fan,N-CDs was measured on the fluorescence spectrophotometer with the slit width of 10 nm and 20 nm at 4 min. In order to estimate the coexisting interference, the selectivity of Fan,N-CDs towards various substances, such as metal ions, amino acids, vitamins, anion standard solution and food additives mentioned above, was determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime measurement
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield (QY) of quinine sulfate, which dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4, was specified as a reference. The QY value of quinine sulfate was determined as 54% under excited wavelength of 360 nm. Based on the equation, calculating the QY value of Fan,N-CDs was calculated: Q
CDs = Q
R
(I
CDs
/I
R
) (A
R
/A
CDs
) (η
CDs
2
/η
R
2
).

The calculations of fluorescence lifetime (1) and the fitting model (2) were as follows:
(1)
τ =
∑
A n
τ n
2
A n
τ n
(
n = 1,2,3 … …
)
(2) Y= B + A
1 *exp(-i/τ
1 ) + … + A
n *exp(-i/τ
n ) (n = 1,2,3 … …)

In order to verify the practicality of the purified Fan,N-CDs, energy drinks, carbonated beverage, gum and ketchup samples were chosen. After sonication for 30 min, carbonated beverages were filtered by 0.22 μm membrane. Functional drinks were filtered by 0.22 μm membrane. The 10 g chewing gums were cut into pieces, and 30 mL water was added, then microwaved (60% power, 30 s), mixed for 5 min, centrifuged at 8000 g for 10 min to remove the supernatant, and then filtered. 3 g tomato paste was added into 10 mL MeoH/H2O (V/V, 1:1), for sonication for 10 min, centrifuged at 8000 g for 10 min, and then the supernatant was filtered. At the same time, spiking experiment was performed: a quantity of the standard solution of new coccine was added to the food samples of energy drinks, carbonated beverage, chewing gum and ketchup after treating.
With simple hydrothermal way, three CDs were prepared, and then purified. The purified Fan,N-CDs with high green fluorescence were obtained (Fig. 1 ).

The Fan,N-CDs had uniform spherical particles, with average diameter about 3.42 nm and lattice spacing about 0.21 nm, determined by TEM (Fig. 2 a). The absorption peak at 3424.9 cm−1 indicates O–H or N–H bond; the peak at 1621.8 cm−1 implies CC bond; the peak at 1425.1 cm−1 indicates C–H bond; the peak at 1130 cm−1 reveals C–O–C bond by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2b) [25]. The peak at 21.75 by XRD indicates the form of amorphous carbon structure after graphitization at high temperature (Fig. 2c).

The peaks at 284.8 eV, 398.8 eV and 532.4 eV are attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s by XPS, respectively, (Fig. 3 a). The peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.4 eV and 288.2 eV are associated with CC, C–N/CN and N–CO, respectively (Fig. 3b). The peaks at 401.4 eV and 399.0 eV, correspond to -NH3 and C–N of N1s spectrum, respectively (Fig. 3c) [26]. The peaks at 532.4 eV and 535.4 eV are attributed to O–H and adsorbed O/H2O, respectively (Fig. 3d). XPS diagram is consistent with FTIR spectrum. It can be seen that there are abundant hydrophilic and amino functional groups on Fan,N-CDs, showing good water solubility. Therefore, the Fan,N-CDs present the characteristics of fluorescent nanoprobe and show certain application potential.

After dissolved in deuterated reagent, the secondarily purified Fan,N-CDs were transferred to NMR tube. No signals were detected in 1HNMR and 13CNMR by NMR spectrometer. Spectra was processed by MestReNova software. No signals were detected in the 1HNMR and 13CNMR of Fan,N-CDs (Fig. S1), indicating no molecular structure of the carbon nanocapsules.

Under sunlight, liquids are colorless. Bule, blue-green, and green fluorescence could be observed under UV excitation, indicating that CDs1, CDs2 and CDs3 (Fan,N-CDs) were successfully prepared (Fig. 4 a–c, inset). The absorption peak around 270 nm is attributed to π-π* transitions (CC bond) (Fig. 4a–c) [27]. For CDs1, the emission wavelength of CDs1 is 381 nm, and the optimal excitation wavelength is 319 nm (Fig. 4a). For CDs2 and CD3, two emission wavelengths are 404 nm and 515 nm (Figs. 4b), 404 nm and 515 nm (Fig. 4c), respectively, while the optimal excitation wavelength is both 315 nm (Fig. 4b and c). The ratio fluorescent CDs contain blue and green fluorescence. The polarity of the blue fluorescent CDs is smaller, while that of the green fluorescence is larger. They could be separated by column chromatography based on polarity. CDs1 shows blue fluorescence, therefore there is only one peak. CDs2 and CDs3 belong to ratio fluorescence which is the mixture of the blue and green fluorescence, so there are two peaks. Fluorescence intensity of Fan,N-CDs is enhanced with excitation wavelength shifts (280–320 nm), and then lowered (330–370 nm). Meanwhile, there is a redshift of emission peak, denoting wavelength dependence (Fig. 4d). When measuring the excitation emission spectrum by the FS5 instrument, the detection range is fixed (for example 10 points), so the excitation emission spectrum is not complete in the fluorescence emission spectrum. The phenomenon has also emerged in other reports [28,29].

The stability of Fan,N-CDs fluorescence was studied with different pH, salt concentration, antioxidant performance, and response time under natural light and UV. At the acid condition, Fan,N-CDs fluorescence intensity was relatively stable (Fig. S2a), only fluctuating slightly with the increase of NaCl concentration increasing (0–4.0 mM) (Fig. S2b). There was almost no variation of fluorescence intensity after addition of H2O2, showing better antioxidant performance (Fig. S2c). No significant change of fluorescence quenching efficiency was found, indicating little influence of reaction time (within 60 min) on the fluorescence response under natural light, and thus strong photostability of Fan,N-CDs (Fig. S2d). The fluorescence of Fan,N-CDs was relatively stable under UV light within 60 min (Fig. S2e).
The measured QY of Fan,N-CDs were 0.39% and 1.28% at emission wavelengths of 404 nm and 515 nm, respectively.

By monitoring the ratiometric fluorescent value of different possible interfering substances, the selectivity of Fan,N-CDs was investigated. Possible interfering substances included metal ions, amino acids, vitamins, anion standard solution and food additives. The analyte and the interference presented at the same time to verify the interference. The concentration of E124 was 25 μm/L, while that of the substances to be tested in the selective test was 100 μm/L. There is two emission peaks in CDs2, among which the first peak emission peak (Em1 = 404) is basically unchanged, while the second emission peak (Em2 = 515) would be quenched when detects E124. When the Em2/Em1 value decreases (F515/F404), that is, proportional fluorescence quenching occurs. Only new coccine could efficiently quench the fluorescence of Fan,N-CDs by ratiometric fluorescent value (Fig. 5 a and b). So, high selectivity was found between Fan,N-CDs and new coccine, and then new coccine could be detected by Fan,N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of new coccine detection based on dual-mode nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence intensity of Fan,N-CDs altered within pH 2.2–12 after addition of new coccine, and the maximum quenching efficiency was obtained at pH 3.0 (Fig. S3a). After addition of new coccine, the fluorescence intensity of Fan,N-CDs remained stable within 4 min (Fig. S3b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanisms of new coccine detection
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, five quenching mechanisms of the quencher to fluorescence CDs have been recognized: static quenching (SQ), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), dynamic quenching (DQ), inner filter effect (IFE) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [41].

UV–vis absorption of Fan,N-CDs, new coccine and Fan,N-CDs with new coccine was measured (Fig. 7 a). When a nonfluorescent ground-state complex is formed between CDs and quencher, static quenching occurs [42]. There was no change with or without new coccine of Fan,N-CDs absorption, so there was not new complex which was formed. Hence, SQ process was denied [42]. For static quenching and IFE, τ
0
/τ = 1. There was no enhancement without (7.68 ns) or with new coccine (7.74 ns) of fluorescence decay lifetime (Fig. 7b). So, IFE may work [42]. When the absorption spectrum of quencher overlapped with the excitation or emission spectra of CDs, IFE occurs. The absorption spectrum of new coccine overlapped with the excitation spectrum of Fan,N-CDs (Fig. 7c), which induces the occurrence of IFE and the fluorescence quenching of a part of Fan,N-CDs [42].

To prove IFE, Parker equation, as follows, was used [42].
F
c o r
F
o b s d
=
2.3 d A
e x
1 − 10
− d
A
e x
10
g A
e m
2.3 s A
e m
1 − 10
− s A
e m

With the increase of new coccine concentrations from 0 to 25 μM, F
cor
/F
obsd value increased (1.122–1.943) (Fig. S4). It was in accordance with the Parker equation. Hence, it was verified that the quenching mechanism was IFE. According to the report by Liu et al., the sensing mechanism based on the fluorescence quenching of N,P-CQDs by new coccine was also IFE [30].

The fluorescent nanoprobe Fan,N-CDs were used to determine new coccine in the real energy drinks, carbonated beverage, gum and ketchup samples. The recoveries of new coccine were estimated to fall within 97.6–104.7% based on fluorescence and 95.7–102.7% based on UV absorbance, respectively (Table 2 ), presenting the sensibility of Fan,N-CDs on new coccine in energy drinks, carbonated beverage, gum and ketchup (n = 3). In addition, the recovery rate of new coccine with Fan,N-CDs based on fluorescence was higher than that based on HPLC. Moreover, HPLC requires complex solution configuration. Therefore, fluorescent Fan,N-CDs were a better option to determine new coccine in real samples.

The highly green fluorescent nanosensor Fan,N-CDs was prepared and purified. 1HNMR and 13CNMR of the purified Fan,N-CDs indicated no molecular structure of the purified carbon nanocapsules. Fan,N-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence characters. In addition, new coccine could be detected by dual-mode nanosensing method with excellent linear ranges and LOD based on ratiometric fluorescent and UV absorption of Fan,N-CDs. Furthermore, the dual-mode nanosensing platform was found efficiently applicable to determine new coccine in energy drinks, carbonated beverage, gum and ketchup samples. Thus, due to simple synthesis, low cost, and high selectivity and sensitivity, green ratiometric fluorescent Fan,N-CDs were a good candidate to detect new coccine.

Yujiao Tu: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Ze Liu: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Lei Jiang: Data curation. Yingying Xiang: Data curation. Fei Song: Investigation, Validation. Lifen Meng: Investigation, Validation. Xiuling Ji: Writing-Reviewing and Editing. Lin Yuan: Writing-Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support from the Science and Technology Planning Project of Yunnan Science and Technology Department Joint Project of Local Colleges and Universities (202001BA070001-218).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Food Addit Contam Part A Chem Anal Control Expo Risk Assess
